channel.title: arXiv keyword digest
channel.description: Daily arXiv papers summarized with Codex

item.title: Attention-Guided Saliency Maps for Interpreting Visualization Literacy in VLMs
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 65, Empirical 42, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations and institutional signals, author citation counts or h-index, DOI or journal reference, numerical evaluation results, named datasets and scenario coverage, models evaluated

Score evidence: The paper is arXiv version 1 and is marked for presentation at IEEE VIS 2026.; The method aggregates language-model attention across all heads and layers and maps it to the vision encoder patch grid.; The saliency maps provide a correspondence between generated answer tokens and image regions.; Evaluation uses a deletion metric intended to validate causal faithfulness.

TL;DR: The paper introduces a fast, gradient-free method that projects a VLM’s language-model attention onto image patches to explain which visualization regions influenced each generated answer token.

Problem: It studies how vision-language models interpret data visualizations and how researchers can diagnose whether generated answers attend to semantically relevant visual elements.

Method: The method aggregates attention from generated language tokens to visual tokens across transformer heads and layers, projects the result onto the vision encoder’s patch grid, and produces token-specific saliency maps; causal faithfulness is assessed using a deletion metric.

Key contribution: A lightweight diagnostic tailored to image-conditioned text generation that directly links individual output tokens to attended visualization regions without requiring gradients.

Main result: The abstract says deletion-metric evaluation validates the maps’ causal faithfulness to model behavior, but provides no numerical results, datasets, model coverage, baselines, or statistical analysis.

Limitation: Attention weights are not automatically explanations of model reasoning, and the abstract does not establish semantic correctness beyond deletion-based behavioral sensitivity. Comparative baselines, ablations, robustness tests, quantitative effect sizes, code, and data availability are not reported.

Relevance: The match to “causality” is meaningful but narrow: causality appears through a deletion metric testing whether highlighted regions causally affect model behavior, rather than through causal inference, causal discovery, or a general causal modeling framework.

Tags: vision-language models, saliency maps, transformer attention, data visualization, interpretability, causal faithfulness, deletion metric, IEEE VIS 2026

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing output-token attention aggregation across transformer heads and layers, projection onto the vision patch grid, and the resulting visualization saliency map would be useful; no direct figure URL is supplied.

Authors: Maeve Hutchinson, Abderrahmane Wassim Mehdaoui, Pranava Madhyastha

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: How Do VLMs Fail? Vision-Operation Misalignment in Compositional VQA
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 70, Empirical 50, Reproducibility 15, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations and institutional information, author citation counts and h-indexes, DOI or journal reference, evaluated VLM names and model count, datasets, scenarios, and sample sizes, quantitative metrics and effect sizes

Score evidence: The manuscript is arXiv version 1, published July 17, 2026.; The metadata states that it was accepted at ACM Multimedia 2026.; The abstract explicitly identifies four failure modes.; Three complementary causal interventions are reported as being applied across all transformer layers.

TL;DR: The paper presents an operation-centric mechanistic framework for diagnosing compositional-VQA failures in VLMs and uses causal interventions to associate distinct failure modes with different transformer pathways.

Problem: It studies why Vision-Language Models fail on compositional visual question answering, where answers require multiple operations such as object selection, spatial-relation resolution, and attribute verification.

Method: The authors decompose errors along two axes: the reasoning operation where an error originates and the internal computational pathway through which it propagates. They identify four failure modes and apply three complementary causal interventions across transformer layers to localize their computational routes.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a mechanistic dissociation among error types: grounding failures are attributed exclusively to feedforward networks, reasoning failures to late-layer attention, and attribute-extraction failures to answer-position feedforward computation, suggesting that VLM corrections should be failure-specific rather than uniform.

Main result: The analysis reports four mechanistically distinct modes—grounding, reasoning, attribute-extraction, and language-prior-dominance failures—with different relationships between visual-grounding strength and answer correctness. No quantitative accuracy changes, effect sizes, model count, dataset coverage, or statistical results are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not identify the evaluated VLMs, datasets, sample size, metrics, intervention controls, quantitative effect sizes, robustness tests, baselines, or public code. The strong pathway-localization claims therefore cannot be assessed for generality or reproducibility from the supplied information.

Relevance: The paper strongly matches the query “causality” because causal interventions are central to its mechanistic analysis of how failures arise and propagate through VLM transformer components, although it is about causal model analysis rather than causal inference from observational data.

Tags: vision-language models, compositional VQA, mechanistic interpretability, causal interventions, failure analysis, visual grounding, transformers, multimedia reasoning

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the operation-centric framework, mapping the four failure modes to their originating reasoning operations and their propagation pathways through attention and feedforward components across transformer layers.

Authors: Navya Gupta, Bingjie Xu, Avinash Anand, Timothy Liu, Zhengchen Zhang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Improving Mendelian Randomization Analysis by Instrument Borrowing from Auxiliary Outcome Traits
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 50, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institution information, Author citation counts and h-index information, DOI, journal, or conference status, Paper citation and influential-citation signals, Open-source code or repository, Public data and detailed implementation information

Score evidence: The paper is a first-version arXiv submission in stat.ME.; It introduces a coheterogeneity statistic and states that asymptotic theory is developed under multiple paradigms.; It extends MR-Mode and MR-PRESSO using a secondary outcome trait.; The abstract reports extensive simulations and applications to positive and negative controls.

TL;DR: The paper improves Mendelian randomization by identifying auxiliary outcome traits that likely share valid genetic instruments with a primary outcome and borrowing those instruments to make causal estimates more robust and powerful.

Problem: Standard Mendelian randomization methods can become biased or lose statistical power when some genetic instruments are invalid; the paper studies whether information from closely related outcome traits can mitigate this problem.

Method: The authors introduce instrument borrowing across outcome traits, develop a coheterogeneity statistic with asymptotic theory to select secondary traits likely to share valid instruments with the primary trait, and extend MR-Mode and MR-PRESSO to incorporate a selected secondary outcome.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a formal mechanism for detecting cross-trait overlap in valid instruments and exploiting that overlap within established robust MR procedures, rather than analyzing each outcome independently.

Main result: The abstract reports that extensive simulations show the coheterogeneity statistic has the expected finite-sample behavior and that instrument-borrowing variants consistently outperform standard MR-Mode and MR-PRESSO, with larger gains as valid-instrument overlap increases. Positive and negative control applications reportedly show better type-I-error control and higher power, but no numerical effect sizes or uncertainty estimates are provided.

Limitation: The abstract provides no quantitative performance results, dataset sizes, detailed baseline coverage, ablations, robustness or sensitivity analyses, code, or public-data links. Benefits also appear conditional on substantial sharing of valid instruments across outcomes, while the consequences of incorrectly selecting an auxiliary trait are not described.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it proposes and evaluates a new framework for more reliable causal inference using Mendelian randomization and genetic instrumental variables.

Tags: causality, Mendelian randomization, instrumental variables, invalid instruments, instrument borrowing, coheterogeneity, MR-Mode, MR-PRESSO

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing auxiliary-trait screening with the coheterogeneity statistic, shared-valid-instrument selection, and the instrument-borrowing extensions of MR-Mode and MR-PRESSO would be useful; no direct figure URL is supplied.

Authors: Anagh Chattopadhyay, Nilanjan Chatterjee

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Induction in Both Directions: A Mechanistic Analysis of In-Context Learning in Masked Diffusion Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 45, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: author affiliations and institution quality, author citation counts and h-indices, paper citation and influential-citation signals, DOI, conference, or journal acceptance, quantitative metrics and effect sizes, datasets, scenarios, and model scales

Score evidence: The study compares attention-only autoregressive models and absorbing-mask diffusion models with matched architectures.; The abstract identifies previous-token, next-token, and later induction heads as components of the proposed circuit.; Direction symmetry is tested by placing the matching source in the past or future.; Restricting visibility to left context removes the diffusion model's reported induction advantage.

TL;DR: The paper identifies a direction-symmetric, bidirectional induction circuit in masked diffusion language models and presents causal evidence that these models infer the denoising timestep from the global masked-token fraction.

Problem: It studies how diffusion language models implement induction—the repeated-context matching and token-copying mechanism associated with in-context learning—and how their mechanism differs from autoregressive transformers.

Method: The authors compare matched-architecture, attention-only autoregressive models and absorbing-mask diffusion language models, analyze their attention heads and residual-stream computations, and apply causal interventions to test both induction circuitry and implicit timestep computation.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a mechanistic account of bidirectional induction: previous-token and next-token heads encode local context, after which induction heads locate a matching source on either side of the masked position and copy its answer. The paper also reports that the global fraction of masked tokens acts as an implicit timestep despite the absence of an explicit timestep embedding.

Main result: The diffusion model does not outperform its autoregressive counterpart when restricted to left context, but exhibits stronger induction when context on both sides of a masked token is available; this attributes its advantage to bidirectional access rather than a stronger one-sided induction mechanism.

Limitation: The abstract provides no quantitative effect sizes, benchmark or dataset details, statistical robustness tests, broad model-scale evaluation, code, public data, or comparison beyond matched attention-only models. Generalization to modern full-scale language models is therefore unclear.

Relevance: The paper directly invokes causal evidence and mechanistic interventions to establish how diffusion language models compute induction and implicit timesteps, making it relevant to the causality query, although its primary subject is mechanistic interpretability rather than causal inference methodology.

Tags: diffusion language models, mechanistic interpretability, in-context learning, induction circuits, causal interventions, bidirectional attention, implicit timestep

Figure note: A circuit diagram would be useful, showing previous-token and next-token heads writing bidirectional local context into the residual stream, followed by induction heads matching a source position and copying its answer.

Authors: Andy Catruna, Emilian Radoi

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Before the Action: Benchmarking LLMs on Prospective Hypothesis Discovery
item.description:
Priority: 68/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 39, Paper 70, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 85, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institution information, Author citation counts and h-index information, DOI, journal, or conference publication signal, Numerical benchmark results and model-specific scores, Agreement coefficients and statistical uncertainty, Detailed baselines and component ablations

Score evidence: HypoData contains 988 cases spanning six scientific and analytical domains.; Experiments cover 15 frontier LLMs.; Evaluation combines pairwise arena judgments with Bradley–Terry–Davidson aggregation and six-dimensional rubric scoring.; Aggregated rankings reportedly show strong agreement with human experts and an independent judge.

TL;DR: The paper introduces Prospective Hypothesis Discovery and HypoArena to evaluate whether LLMs can generate grounded, discriminative, and testable hypotheses from inconclusive evidence before a final conclusion is known.

Problem: LLM evaluations generally test answers to pre-specified questions, leaving their ability to formulate useful investigative hypotheses from anomalous observations and fragmented records largely unmeasured.

Method: HypoArena combines HypoData, a benchmark of 988 cases across six scientific and analytical domains, with HypoEval, which uses bidirectional pairwise judgments, Bradley–Terry–Davidson ranking, and a six-dimensional diagnostic rubric. Cases are produced through Retrospective Context Regression, a Forge–Audit pipeline that removes conclusions, target hypotheses, and retrospective causal claims from completed expert documents while retaining their factual evidence.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a dedicated evaluation target for pre-conclusion hypothesis-space construction, supported by a scalable pipeline for reconstructing inconclusive contexts and an arena-style evaluator designed to accommodate multiple valid hypothesis sets.

Main result: Across 15 frontier LLMs, the benchmark reveals clear capability stratification. Structured analytical skills improve several lower-performing models but cause regressions in others, including a top-performing model. Arena rankings distinguish models more finely than absolute rubric scores and reportedly agree strongly with human experts and an independent judge; the abstract provides no numerical effect sizes or agreement coefficients.

Limitation: The abstract does not report model-level scores, effect sizes, uncertainty estimates, ablations of the data-construction and evaluation components, or detailed human-evaluation protocols. Reconstructing pre-conclusion cases from documents written after conclusions were reached may also introduce retrospective leakage or selection bias, though the Forge–Audit process is intended to reduce it.

Relevance: The paper matches the causality query because it evaluates hypothesis construction from evidence and explicitly removes retrospective causal attributions when reconstructing investigative contexts. Its connection is primarily to causal reasoning and discovery evaluation rather than to a new formal causal-inference estimator or identification method.

Tags: large-language-models, hypothesis-discovery, causal-reasoning, benchmark, open-ended-evaluation, Bradley-Terry-Davidson, scientific-discovery

Figure note: A useful image would diagram the Retrospective Context Regression Forge–Audit pipeline from completed expert documents to conclusion-withheld cases, followed by HypoEval pairwise judging, Bradley–Terry–Davidson aggregation, and rubric-based diagnosis.

Authors: Tianyun Zhong, Wangyi Jiang, Wei Wang, Xuanang Chen, Yaojie Lu, Shiwei Ye, Yuzhen Shi, Boyu Yang, Jinghang Wang, Han Li, Weiqi Zhai, Bing Zhao, Hu Wei, Haiyang Yu, Yongbin Li, Hongyu Lin, Le Sun, Xianpei Han

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: A cubical formalisation of topos causal models: intervention, sheaf gluing, and the intuitionistic do-calculus
item.description:
Priority: 62/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 65, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 90, Manuscript 88

Missing quality signals: author affiliation and institution, author citation count and h-index, paper citation and influential-citation data, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, quantitative empirical benchmarks and baselines, ablations and statistical robustness

Score evidence: The paper is arXiv v1, published 2026-07-17, with no DOI or journal reference supplied.; The abstract claims the first machine-checked account of the framework’s 1-topos core.; The development reportedly assumes no axioms and typechecks under Agda’s --safe flag.; A public GitHub repository is included in the metadata.

TL;DR: This paper gives the first machine-checked Cubical Agda formalization of the presheaf 1-topos core of topos causal models, repairing a modal-layer axiom gap and adding a contextuality obstruction.

Problem: Topos causal models offer a categorical and intuitionistic foundation for causal inference, but central claims about interventions, sheaf gluing, internal-language semantics, and modal stability had not been fully machine-checked; one claimed Lawvere–Tierney axiom set is also insufficient to induce a closure operator.

Method: The author formalizes causal worlds as presheaves and interventions as characteristic maps into a sieve-based subobject classifier in Cubical Agda, building on a verified probability monad and do-calculus. The development proves intervention classification, independent-mechanism sheaf gluing, Kripke–Joyal forcing clauses, topology-stability of interventions and Pearl’s rules, and a contextuality obstruction to global-model construction.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a safe, axiom-free machine-checked account of the framework’s 1-topos core. It supplies an omitted sheaf-gluing proof, identifies and repairs a missing Lawvere–Tierney law, connects counterfactual transportability across regime covers to j-stability, and formalizes pairwise-compatible local causal data that have no global model.

Main result: The Agda development typechecks with the --safe flag and concretely instantiates the ordered field at the rationals. It verifies the stated classification, gluing, forcing, stability, and contextuality results; the abstract reports no conventional predictive benchmark or quantitative empirical comparison.

Limitation: The work covers only the presheaf 1-topos fragment; type-level sheafification and the directed lift are deferred. The abstract provides no experimental evaluation, baseline comparison, ablation, robustness analysis, citation evidence, affiliation, or peer-reviewed venue signal.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the “causal inference” query and is especially relevant to researchers interested in formal foundations of interventions, do-calculus, categorical causality, proof assistants, transportability, or contextuality. It is less immediately useful for readers seeking applied causal-estimation algorithms or benchmark improvements.

Tags: causal inference, topos causal models, Cubical Agda, formal verification, do-calculus, category theory, sheaf theory, Lawvere–Tierney topologyvity_obstruction

Figure note: A useful image would diagram the formalization pipeline from presheaf causal worlds through sieve classifiers and characteristic-map interventions to sheaf gluing, modal j-stability, transportability, and the contextuality obstruction.

Authors: Karen Sargsyan

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: Information-Directed Sampling for Causal Bandits
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 65, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institutional signals, Author citation counts and h-indices, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, Paper citation and influential-citation information, Open-source code or repository, Public datasets

Score evidence: arXiv v1 submitted in July 2026 in cs.LG and cs.AI.; The abstract states known-graph and no-latent-confounding assumptions.; Entropy-dependent Bayesian regret bounds are claimed for causal Thompson Sampling and IDS.; The IDS analysis explicitly includes Monte Carlo approximation error and supplies high-probability confidence bounds.

TL;DR: The paper extends contextual causal bandits to settings with non-manipulable pre- and post-intervention variables, proposing causal Thompson Sampling and Information-Directed Sampling algorithms with entropy-dependent Bayesian regret guarantees.

Problem: It studies how a bandit learner can identify high-reward interventions when some causally relevant variables cannot be manipulated but can be observed either before action selection as context or after an intervention.

Method: Under a known causal graph with no latent confounding, the authors use a Bayesian model whose unknown parameters are the observational distribution's conditional probability tables. Evidence from one intervention updates estimates for other interventions through shared causal mechanisms. They develop causal versions of Thompson Sampling and IDS, including Monte Carlo estimation and confidence bounds for IDS quantities.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a framework that exploits both non-manipulable contextual and post-intervention variables for cross-intervention information sharing. The paper also gives an IDS regret analysis that explicitly accounts for Monte Carlo error in expected-regret and information-gain estimates, alongside an entropy-dependent Thompson Sampling bound.

Main result: The abstract reports entropy-dependent sublinear Bayesian regret for Thompson Sampling and IDS, with the exact-computation IDS case recovering the standard sublinear rate. On several synthetic causal-bandit tasks, both proposed methods outperform causal and non-causal baselines, but no numerical improvements or uncertainty statistics are provided.

Limitation: The model assumes a known causal graph and no latent confounding. Evidence is limited in the abstract to synthetic tasks; it reports no quantitative effect sizes, real-world evaluation, ablations, robustness tests, statistical significance, runtime analysis, code, or public data.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query "causality": causal structure is central to its bandit algorithms, Bayesian updates, theoretical regret analysis, and empirical comparisons.

Tags: causality, causal bandits, information-directed sampling, Thompson sampling, contextual bandits, Bayesian regret, Monte Carlo approximation

Figure note: A diagram of the assumed causal graph and decision timeline—pre-action context, intervention, post-intervention observations, reward, and cross-intervention Bayesian updates—would clarify the framework.

Authors: Muhammad Qasim Elahi, Murat Kocaoglu, Mahsa Ghasemi

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Ask Twice, Look Twice: Prompt Echoing Resolves the Question-First Paradox in Vision-Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 23, Paper 55, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 88

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institutional signals, Author citation counts and h-indices, DOI, conference, or journal acceptance, Paper citation and influential-citation data, Open-source code or repository, Public experiment artifacts or implementation details

Score evidence: The abstract reports experiments across five open VLMs.; Evaluation names NaturalBench, POPE, Winoground, and open-ended VQAv2.; The question-first penalty reaches 17.5 group-accuracy points.; Prompt echoing gains up to 19 Winoground group-accuracy points.

TL;DR: The paper explains why placing a question before an image can hurt VLM accuracy despite improving question-guided perception, and proposes training-free prompt echoing to preserve both perceptual steering and downstream question access.

Problem: It studies the “question-first paradox”: question-first prompts steer visual representations toward relevant concepts but consistently underperform image-first prompts on visual question answering benchmarks.

Method: The authors use logit-lens and attention probes plus a causal attention-knockout intervention to separate perception-time steering from answer-time question access, then introduce question echoing—placing the question both before and after the image—and optionally image echoing.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a two-stage mechanistic account of prompt-order effects: question-first placement improves perception but strands the question behind many image tokens during decoding. Echoing assigns one question copy to perceptual steering and another to answer readout, requiring no training or architecture changes.

Main result: Across five open VLMs, question-first prompting loses up to 17.5 group-accuracy points. Echoed prompts close the gap and outperform the best single-pass ordering on NaturalBench, POPE, Winoground, and open-ended VQAv2, including gains of up to 19 Winoground group-accuracy points.

Limitation: The abstract does not report model names, detailed per-model results, statistical uncertainty, systematic ablations, robustness to prompt wording or context length, computational overhead from echoing, or evidence from closed/frontier VLMs. No code or repository is identified.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the “causality” query through its causal attention-knockout experiment, which tests whether answer tokens causally depend on access to the question. Its broader contribution is mechanistic causal analysis of prompt ordering rather than development of a general causal-inference method.

Tags: vision-language models, causality, causal intervention, attention knockout, prompt ordering, prompt echoing, visual question answering, mechanistic interpretability

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the two-stage mechanism: a pre-image question steering image-patch representations, loss of question access across the image-token span, and echoed post-image question enabling answer-time readout.

Authors: Rakshanda Hassan Abhinandan, John Galeotti, Deva Ramanan, Gautam Rajendrakumar Gare

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Hierarchical Denoising For Multi-Step Visual Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 55, Empirical 80, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 86

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institutional signals, Author citation counts and h-indices, DOI, journal, or conference venue, Independent citation or influential-citation evidence, Open-source code and repository quality, Confirmed public benchmark or training data

Score evidence: The submission is arXiv v1, published July 16, 2026, with no DOI or journal reference supplied.; The benchmark covers six tasks and explicitly includes out-of-distribution cases.; Success improves from 34.22 to 60.29, with average progress increasing from 76.00 to 89.56.; Inference is reported at 0.70 seconds per latent and 54.2 times faster than bidirectional diffusion.

TL;DR: HDR uses tree-structured hierarchical video latents and sparse attention to plan multi-step visual solutions globally before producing low-latency causal streaming outputs.

Problem: The paper studies how video diffusion models can perform logically consistent multi-step visual reasoning while retaining the low inference latency required for streaming generation.

Method: HDR integrates a tree-structured hierarchy of latents into causal video generation: coarse denoising levels retain alternative hypotheses for global planning, finer levels convert them into concrete visual states, and a sparse hierarchical attention pattern reduces temporal attention cost. The authors also introduce a level-stratified benchmark spanning six puzzle and planning tasks with out-of-distribution cases.

Key contribution: The central novelty is combining coarse-to-fine, globally planned hierarchical denoising with causal streaming generation, aiming to bridge the reasoning–latency tradeoff between autoregressive and bidirectional diffusion models.

Main result: Against streaming autoregressive diffusion baselines, HDR raises success from 34.22 to 60.29 and average progress from 76.00 to 89.56. It reports 0.70 seconds per latent and 54.2-times faster inference than bidirectional diffusion, while retaining 82.9% of full-data performance using 2% of the training data. Real-world robot experiments are mentioned but not quantified in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract provides no statistical uncertainty, named baselines, ablations, robustness analysis, or implementation details. It does not establish that the benchmark, code, or data are publicly available, and the scope and quantitative results of the robot experiments are unclear.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query "causality" is limited: the paper uses causal generation in the temporal/autoregressive sense, but the abstract does not address causal inference, causal discovery, interventions, or counterfactual reasoning.

Tags: video diffusion, visual reasoning, hierarchical denoising, causal video generation, streaming generation, sparse attention, planning benchmarks, robotics

Figure note: A framework diagram showing HDR's tree-structured latent hierarchy, coarse-to-fine denoising flow, sparse hierarchical attention, and transition into causal streaming output would be useful.

Authors: Zezhong Qian, Xiaowei Chi, Chak-Wing Mak, Tianze Zhou, Ruibin Yuan, Yuhan Rui, Hengzhe Sun, Zhuoqun Wu, Yuming Li, Siyuan Qian, Sirui Han, Shanghang Zhang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: teLLMe Why (Ain't Nothing but a Jam): Exploratory Causal Analysis of Urban Driving Data
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 45, Empirical 35, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institutional metadata, Author citation counts and h-index information, Paper citation and influential-citation information, DOI, journal reference, or archival conference publication, Open-source code or repository, Release details for the derived event table or processed data

Score evidence: The pipeline explicitly uses the PC algorithm for causal structure learning.; Bootstrap-based stability checks are applied to learned causal structure.; Effect estimation uses linear regression and DoWhy.; A schema-aware LLM maps questions to treatments, outcomes, and subpopulations.

TL;DR: teLLMe is an exploratory system that turns natural-language questions about observational urban-driving data into structured causal analyses combining PC-based structure learning, stability checks, effect estimation, and uncertainty-aware reporting.

Problem: Traffic agencies want to answer counterfactual questions—such as how rain would affect traffic density—from observational video-derived datasets in which interventions and randomized treatment assignments are unavailable.

Method: The system constructs a structured event table from dashcam annotations, learns causal structure with the PC algorithm, evaluates graph stability through bootstrapping, and estimates query-specific effects using linear regression and DoWhy. A schema-aware LLM maps natural-language questions to treatments, outcomes, and subpopulations. Results are presented as a Causal Card containing estimates, adjustment sets, DAG support, assumptions, and a short explanation.

Key contribution: The main novelty is an integrated, user-facing causal exploration workflow that connects schema-constrained natural-language querying with causal discovery, stability analysis, effect estimation, and explicit reporting of assumptions for urban-driving datasets; the abstract does not establish that any individual causal method is technically new.

Main result: Case studies using BDD-derived traffic events reportedly surface plausible relationships among weather, peak hours, and traffic density. The abstract supplies no quantitative effect sizes, accuracy measures, baseline comparisons, or statistical robustness results.

Limitation: The system relies on observational data, learned causal graphs, linear regression, and modeling assumptions, so it cannot establish definitive causal effects. Evidence is limited to unspecified case studies, and the abstract reports no ground-truth validation, comparative baselines, ablations, sensitivity analysis, or numerical performance results.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query “causal structure learning”: PC-based causal discovery is a core pipeline component, supplemented by bootstrap stability checks and downstream causal-effect estimation. It is especially relevant for researchers interested in applied, human-facing causal-analysis systems rather than new structure-learning algorithms.

Tags: causal structure learning, causal discovery, PC algorithm, causal effect estimation, DoWhy, urban driving, traffic analysis, natural-language interfaces

Figure note: A pipeline figure would be useful showing the path from dashcam annotations and the structured event table through LLM query parsing, PC structure learning, bootstrap stability analysis, DoWhy effect estimation, and the final Causal Card.

Authors: Qiwei Li, Jorge Ortiz

Query: causal structure learning, causality

PDF

item.title: Bridge Evidence: Static Retrieval Utility Does Not Predict Causal Utility in Multi-Step Agentic Search
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 52, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institution information, Author citation counts and h-indexes, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, Paper citation or influential-citation information, Open-source code or repository, Released trajectory/intervention data

Score evidence: The study replays 1,000 HotpotQA development questions and analyzes 23,322 document observations.; The primary comparison reports Spearman rho = -0.026 between CTU and SRU.; A proxy-based robustness check reports a 27.2% bridge cell.; The mechanism experiment covers 227,139 entity observations and reports a 4.02-fold next-query difference.

TL;DR: The paper shows that static document relevance is nearly unrelated to a document’s causal value in multi-step agentic search, because seemingly irrelevant “bridge documents” can reveal entities that redirect later queries.

Problem: Standard retrieval evaluation scores documents by whether they directly help answer the current question, potentially missing documents whose value emerges through their effect on later searches and reasoning steps.

Method: The authors introduce Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU): for each document read by a ReAct-style agent on 1,000 HotpotQA development questions, they delete the document, rerun the remaining trajectory, and measure changes in final-answer quality, next-query retrieval quality, and turn count. They compare CTU with Static RAG Utility (SRU) across 23,322 document observations and use Observable Entity Relevance to test the proposed entity-mediated mechanism.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a trajectory-level, intervention-based definition of retrieval utility that distinguishes causal usefulness from static relevance and identifies “bridge documents” whose main value is enabling the agent’s next search action.

Main result: CTU and SRU are almost statistically independent (Spearman rho = -0.026). Roughly one-third of read documents are causally important despite appearing statically useless; the bridge category remains 27.2% when static utility is replaced with BM25 and cross-encoder proxies. Discriminative entities appear in the next query 4.02 times as often as entities found only in non-relevant documents (6.1% versus 1.5%, n = 227,139).

Limitation: Evidence in the abstract is limited to a ReAct-style agent, HotpotQA, and 1,000 development questions. It does not report additional datasets, agent architectures, retrieval environments, uncertainty estimates for the bridge proportions, ablations of CTU components, computational cost, or validation that optimizing CTU improves a trained retrieval system.

Relevance: The match to “causality” is direct and substantive: the paper uses document-deletion counterfactuals to estimate causal utility within search trajectories and argues that conventional relevance is not a substitute for causal contribution.

Tags: causality, counterfactual evaluation, agentic retrieval, multi-step search, RAG, information retrieval, ReAct, HotpotQA

Figure note: A useful figure would show an original agent trajectory beside its document-deletion counterfactual, the three CTU deltas, and a CTU-versus-SRU quadrant plot highlighting bridge documents.

Authors: Debayan Mukhopadhyay, Utshab Kumar Ghosh, Shubham Chatterjee

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causal Inference for Sequential Settings under Interference and Latent Confounding
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 55, Empirical 52, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations and institutional metadata, citation and h-index information, DOI, journal, or conference status, open-source code and repository information, public data or preprocessing details, quantitative experimental metrics

Score evidence: The paper is arXiv v1 with no supplied DOI or journal reference.; The abstract states non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation.; Evaluation includes synthetic experiments and a nationwide US-county COVID-19 case study.; The abstract explicitly identifies Markovian outcomes, Ising interference, low-rank latent confounders, and MPLE.

TL;DR: The paper develops a computationally efficient causal-inference method for sequential observational data with network interference and low-rank latent confounding, supported by consistency theory, synthetic experiments, and a US-county COVID-19 vaccine case study.

Problem: It studies estimation of causal effects when binary outcomes evolve over time, outcomes of different units interfere through a network, treatments are observational, and unobserved confounders affect outcomes.

Method: The authors model within-time outcome dependencies using an Ising model, temporal evolution as Markovian, and latent confounders through a low-rank factor structure. They learn model parameters from one high-dimensional sample using maximum pseudo-likelihood estimation, then estimate causal quantities by sampling from the fitted model.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a unified, computationally efficient framework that simultaneously handles sequential dependence, Ising-style outcome interference, and low-rank latent confounding, together with non-asymptotic parameter-consistency guarantees that are connected to causal-estimand accuracy.

Main result: The abstract claims non-asymptotic consistency under mild assumptions and faithful estimation of causal quantities from the learned model. Synthetic experiments and a nationwide US-county study of vaccination rates and COVID-19 death rates are reported, but no quantitative effect sizes, errors, or comparisons are provided.

Limitation: The abstract provides no quantitative experimental results, baseline comparisons, ablations, robustness or sensitivity analyses, uncertainty estimates, or code availability. Causal validity also depends on the assumed Markov, Ising, and low-rank confounding structure, whose plausibility is not evaluated in the supplied information.

Relevance: This is a direct match for the configured causal-inference and causality feed, especially for researchers interested in interference, longitudinal observational data, latent confounding, network outcomes, and theoretically justified causal estimation.

Tags: causal inference, outcome interference, latent confounding, sequential data, observational studies, Ising model, low-rank factor model, maximum pseudo-likelihood estimation

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the pipeline from longitudinal treatments and outcomes through Markov-Ising dependencies and low-rank latent factors, MPLE parameter learning, model-based sampling, and causal-effect estimation.

item.title: FlashDecoder: Real-Time Latent-to-Pixel Streaming Decoder with Transformers
item.description:
Priority: 59/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 39, Paper 72, Empirical 74, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 84

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institution information, Author citation counts and h-indexes, DOI or independently verifiable conference/journal status, Open-source code or repository, Public training and evaluation data details, Ablation studies

Score evidence: The supplied record identifies arXiv v1 and includes the comment “CVPR2026,” but no DOI or journal reference.; The abstract reports experiments on Wan2.1 and Wan2.2 latent spaces.; A concrete 1080p comparison reports 41.55 dB versus 41.49 dB PSNR.; Reported efficiency gains are 3.6–4.7× decoding speed, up to 11× lower memory, and up to 12× speed with inference optimizations.

TL;DR: FlashDecoder replaces slow 3D-convolutional video decoders with a frame-by-frame causal Transformer using a rolling KV cache, reporting comparable reconstruction quality with substantially lower latency and memory use.

Problem: Latent video diffusion systems require fast latent-to-pixel decoding, but conventional 3D-convolutional decoders become slow and memory-intensive for high-resolution or long videos.

Method: A pure-Transformer decoder processes frames sequentially; each frame attends to a fixed window of previous frames through a rolling KV cache, bounding temporal computation and memory independently of video length while enforcing temporal causality through processing order.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a streaming latent-to-pixel Transformer decoder that combines frame-level causal decoding, fixed-window temporal attention, and rolling KV caching to provide bounded memory and constant per-frame latency without explicit causal attention masks.

Main result: Across Wan2.1 and Wan2.2 latent spaces on one H100 GPU, the method reportedly matches convolutional-decoder reconstruction quality—including 41.55 dB versus 41.49 dB PSNR at 1080p—while decoding 3.6–4.7× faster with up to 11× less memory; architecture-aware inference optimization increases the reported speedup to 12×.

Limitation: The abstract reports results only for two related latent spaces and a single H100 setting; it provides no statistical analysis, ablations, perceptual or temporal-consistency metrics, cross-hardware evaluation, code availability, or discussion of possible quality degradation from the fixed temporal window.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query “causality” because temporal causality is a central architectural property: sequential frame decoding prevents access to future frames. Its relevance is primarily to causal streaming video generation, not causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: video generation, latent video diffusion, Transformer decoder, streaming inference, temporal causality, KV cache, efficient decoding, 1080p video

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram sequential latent-to-pixel decoding, showing the current-frame Transformer attending to a fixed window of cached past-frame keys and values while older cache entries are discarded.

item.title: Interventional Causal Circuits for Safe Robot Action Testing and Failure Recovery
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 44, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institution information, Author citation counts and h-indices, Paper citation and influential-citation data, DOI, journal reference, or archival conference acceptance, Open-source code or repository, Public data or reproducible simulation configuration

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-16.; Listed for the 1st IJCAI Workshop on Safe Physical AI at IJCAI 2026.; The abstract reports ROS2 simulation experiments under high-quality and degraded JPT conditions.; Reported failed-attempt reductions are 10.3% and 37%, respectively.

TL;DR: The paper replaces blind resampling after unsafe robot-action rejection with tractable, interpretable causal diagnosis and parameter correction using a Joint Probability Tree coupled to a Causal Circuit.

Problem: Formal safety testing of robot motion parameters is computationally expensive in high-dimensional action spaces, while blindly resampling rejected actions is inefficient and provides neither diagnostic information nor convergence guarantees.

Method: A closed-loop framework combines a Joint Probability Tree with a Causal Circuit derived from a Marginal-Deterministic Variable Tree. It answers interventional queries exactly in polynomial time, validates query tractability before deployment, excludes out-of-support candidates, and reports the causal parameter, observed value, and recommended corrective region for each rejected plan.

Key contribution: The central novelty is treating test rejection as an interventional causal-diagnosis problem rather than a trigger for blind resampling or a separately trained failure model; the framework provides exact corrections without retraining or additional data collection.

Main result: In ROS2 simulation, the Causal Circuit reduced failed attempts by 10.3% with a high-quality JPT and reduced total failed attempts by 37% with a degraded JPT. The abstract does not report absolute counts, uncertainty estimates, or statistical tests.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to unspecified ROS2 simulations and two stated distribution-quality conditions. The abstract provides no named baselines beyond naive resampling, ablations, statistical robustness, real-robot validation, runtime measurements, code, or public data; performance also depends on JPT quality and excludes out-of-support candidates rather than correcting them.

Relevance: This is a strong direct match for the query “causality”: it applies interventional causal inference and causal circuits to safety testing and failure recovery in robot action planning.

Tags: causality, interventional inference, causal circuits, robot safety, failure recovery, Joint Probability Trees, ROS2, physical AI

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing action proposal, formal tester rejection, JPT inference, Causal Circuit diagnosis, out-of-support filtering, corrective-region selection, and retesting would clarify the closed-loop architecture.

item.title: Rare Concept Generation via Counterfactual Inference in Diffusion Models
item.description:

Score evidence: The paper explicitly proposes Counterfactual Inference-based Diffusion, or CI-Diff.; The method uses the Natural Direct Effect and reformulates classifier-free guidance.; Evaluation is reported on the RareBench benchmark.; The abstract claims superiority over state-of-the-art diffusion models without numerical results.

TL;DR: CI-Diff applies counterfactual causal inference to diffusion guidance, aiming to suppress common-attribute bias and improve images depicting objects with unusual attribute combinations.

Problem: The paper studies rare concept generation: text-conditioned image synthesis where objects have atypical attributes that diffusion models often render incorrectly because their training data strongly associates objects with common attributes.

Method: CI-Diff models common-knowledge bias as causal interference and uses the Natural Direct Effect to isolate the rare-concept prompt's influence on image generation. It operationalizes this idea by reformulating classifier-free guidance to emphasize atypical attributes.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is the introduction of counterfactual causal inference, specifically Natural Direct Effect reasoning, into rare concept generation rather than relying solely on standard classifier-free guidance or prompt conditioning.

Main result: The abstract reports that CI-Diff outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion models on RareBench, but provides no numerical improvements, named baselines, per-metric results, or statistical evidence.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not report quantitative results, ablations, robustness tests, computational cost, failure cases, or evaluations beyond RareBench, so the strength and generality of the claimed improvement cannot be assessed.

Relevance: This is highly relevant to the matched query "causal inference" because causal inference is central to the proposed diffusion-generation method rather than merely appearing in background discussion.

Tags: causal inference, counterfactual inference, diffusion models, rare concept generation, classifier-free guidance, Natural Direct Effect, text-to-image generation, computer vision

Figure note: A useful image would be the CI-Diff causal diagram and generation pipeline showing the common-knowledge-bias path, the counterfactual intervention, Natural Direct Effect estimation, and the modified classifier-free guidance branches.

item.title: WorkDrive: Roadwork Chain of Causation for Autonomous Driving
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 48, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 43, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations and institutional provenance, author citation counts and h-indexes, venue, DOI, or journal reference, paper citation and influential-citation information, absolute ADE values and metric completeness, multiple competitive baselines

Score evidence: The submission is arXiv v1 with no DOI, journal reference, or conference information.; Evaluation is reported on ROADWork against a trajectory-only baseline.; The abstract reports a 9.0% ADE reduction from CoC training and a further 3.0% from GRPO.; The method combines structured perception, supervised fine-tuning, and a trajectory-consistency reward.

TL;DR: WorkDrive improves autonomous-driving VLM trajectory prediction in roadwork zones by grounding chain-of-causation annotations in structured perception and optimizing agreement between lateral driving actions and predicted trajectories.

Problem: Autonomous-driving VLMs recognize temporary roadwork devices but often rely on familiar pretraining cues and fail to translate altered lanes, cones, and barriers into appropriate planning decisions.

Method: An automated multitask perception pipeline extracts structured scene facts, which guide a Chain-of-Causation annotation pipeline toward work-zone-specific elements. These reasoning labels support supervised fine-tuning, followed by GRPO reinforcement learning using a single reward that measures consistency between lateral meta-actions and predicted trajectories.

Key contribution: The paper connects perception-grounded, domain-specific causal reasoning labels directly to trajectory prediction and adds a planning-consistency reward. Compared with standard trajectory-only training, it explicitly teaches the model how temporary roadwork observations should affect driving actions.

Main result: On ROADWork, described as the largest public work-zone dataset, roadwork Chain-of-Causation training reduces trajectory average displacement error by 9.0%, and consistency-based GRPO provides a further 3.0% improvement over the trajectory-only baseline.

Limitation: The abstract reports only relative ADE improvements on one named dataset. It provides no absolute metrics, additional baselines, statistical uncertainty, robustness or safety evaluation, detailed ablations, or evidence that the learned reasoning is causally faithful rather than useful supervised rationale. Code and data are promised but not yet linked.

Relevance: The paper directly matches “causal reasoning” through its Chain-of-Causation framework. It is especially relevant for applied causal-style reasoning in autonomous-driving VLMs, although the abstract does not establish use of formal causal inference, interventions, or identifiable causal models.

Tags: causal reasoning, autonomous driving, vision-language models, roadwork zones, trajectory prediction, chain of causation, reinforcement learning, GRPO

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the WorkDrive pipeline from multitask perception and structured scene facts through Chain-of-Causation annotation, supervised fine-tuning, consistency-based GRPO, and final trajectory prediction.

item.title: Causal-Adversarial Probing of Clinical Covariates for Prostate MRI Grading
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 55, Empirical 80, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and institutional evidence, Author citation counts and h-index information, DOI, journal, conference, or peer-review status, Open-source code or repository, Public data availability, Baseline comparisons

Score evidence: The framework explicitly treats MRI appearance and ISUP grade as observations of latent tumour pathology.; Evaluation uses 2,903 MRI examinations and external validation on 576 patients.; The abstract reports covariate-specific AUC changes and statistical significance.; The manuscript is arXiv version 1 with no supplied DOI or journal reference.

TL;DR: The paper uses causal reasoning and adversarial representation probing to determine whether clinical covariates provide useful disease signal or harmful shortcut information in prostate MRI cancer grading.

Problem: Deep prostate MRI grading models may encode clinical covariates, but it is unclear whether these dependencies reflect tumour pathology, non-generalising shortcuts, or irrelevant information.

Method: The authors model MRI appearance and ISUP grade as observations of latent tumour pathology, then adversarially suppress the decodability of one clinical covariate at a time while preserving grade prediction; changes in predictive performance are used to characterize each covariate as a nuisance correlate, disease-related proxy, or irrelevant variable.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a representation-level causal probing framework that combines a latent-pathology causal interpretation with targeted adversarial suppression, allowing covariate dependence to be evaluated empirically rather than merely assumed.

Main result: On 2,903 prostate MRI examinations with external validation on 576 patients, suppressing age, BMI, and alcohol use improved binary ISUP Grade Group >=2 AUC by 1.23%, 0.84%, and 1.42%, respectively (all p<0.05), whereas suppressing PSA and prostate volume reduced AUC by 1.91% and 7.61% (all p<0.001).

Limitation: The abstract does not establish causal effects through intervention, report comparison baselines, or test joint suppression of correlated covariates. It also omits cohort characteristics, subgroup robustness, implementation details, and evidence that the identified dependencies generalize beyond the reported external dataset.

Relevance: This is a strong match for the query "causal reasoning": it explicitly introduces a causal interpretation of latent pathology, imaging, labels, and clinical covariates, then operationalizes that reasoning through adversarial probing. Its claims concern diagnosing learned dependence rather than full causal identification.

Tags: causal reasoning, causality, adversarial learning, shortcut learning, representation probing, prostate MRI, cancer grading, clinical covariates

Figure note: A useful figure would show the assumed causal graph linking latent tumour pathology, MRI appearance, ISUP grade, and clinical covariates, alongside the adversarial encoder, grade predictor, covariate discriminator, and suppression-based interpretation pipeline.

item.title: Analytic Abduction: Causal Decomposition and Governed Commitment for Human--AI Coordination
item.description:
Priority: 37/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 45, Empirical 38, Reproducibility 15, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliation and institutional evidence, Author citation count and h-index, DOI, journal, or conference signal, Citation and influential-citation signals, Quantitative metrics and complete experimental results, Named baselines and comparative evaluation

Score evidence: The submission is arXiv v1 in cs.AI, published July 16, 2026.; The abstract explicitly defines κ, τ, causal clusters, and a two-level κ*/κ** architecture.; Applications are described in epidemiological crisis decomposition and adversarial cyber-threat analysis.; No DOI, journal reference, conference information, or quantitative findings are supplied.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a risk-sensitive analytic-abduction framework that maintains competing causal decompositions until explicit, stakes-dependent conditions justify commitment.

Problem: It studies how humans and AI systems can infer interacting latent causes from complex observations without greedily selecting a single explanation and thereby risking premature convergence or causal misattribution.

Method: The κ-τ apparatus models epistemic interactions among hypotheses with κ and calibrates commitment to decision stakes with threshold τ. Its central representation is a weighted causal cluster, supported by a two-level architecture using intra-cluster κ* and inter-cluster κ** interactions.

Key contribution: The genuinely distinctive idea is to treat suspended causal decomposition as a governed, legible coordination object: multiple interacting explanations remain available, weighted by plausibility and paired with evidence that could discriminate among them, rather than being collapsed immediately into one answer.

Main result: The abstract reports demonstrations in epidemiological crisis decomposition and adversarial cyber-threat analysis, but provides no quantitative results, metrics, comparisons, or evidence that the framework outperforms standard abductive or causal-reasoning approaches.

Limitation: Evidence appears illustrative rather than rigorously empirical from the abstract. The formal properties, operational definitions of κ and τ, threshold-calibration procedure, scalability, sensitivity, baselines, and effectiveness in real human-AI coordination are not reported.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query through its focus on causal decomposition, interacting latent factors, and safeguards against causal misattribution; it is especially relevant if the feed covers abductive causal reasoning or human-AI decision support.

Tags: causality, analytic abduction, causal decomposition, human-AI coordination, risk-sensitive reasoning, epistemic uncertainty, decision support, cyber threat analysis

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the pipeline from observed state to candidate latent factors, weighted causal clusters, intra-cluster κ* interactions, inter-cluster κ** interactions, stakes-calibrated τ gating, and eventual commitment or continued suspension.

Problem: The paper studies explainable systemic-risk assessment in interconnected financial networks, where conventional models may miss nonlinear default cascades and standard GNNs provide correlational, black-box predictions that are inadequate for regulatory stress testing and intervention planning.

Method: CausalGraphX uses a Graph Attention mechanism to represent institutional vulnerability, adversarial regularization intended to suppress spurious correlations and retain causal drivers, and an optimization procedure that generates sparse counterfactual interventions under specified stress scenarios.

Key contribution: The main claimed novelty is an integrated framework that couples GNN-based cascade prediction with causally motivated representation learning and actionable, institution-level counterfactual explanations, rather than applying post-hoc feature attribution to an otherwise correlational predictor.

Main result: On large-scale synthetic financial networks, the framework reportedly significantly outperforms traditional and deep-learning baselines in predicting cascading defaults and produces sparse, plausible, actionable counterfactuals. item.title: Dynamic Manipulation Hypergraphs for HAR: Beyond Pairwise Relations: Dynamic Manipulation Hypergraphs for Vision-Based Human Activity Recognition
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 48, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliation and institution, Author citation count and h-index, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, Paper citation or influential-citation information, Open-source code or repository, Public implementation details beyond the abstract

Score evidence: arXiv v1 in cs.CV, published July 15, 2026.; Controlled comparisons use identical entity inputs for the matched pairwise graph and comparable relational settings for the static hypergraph.; Quantitative results cover EPIC-KITCHENS-100/VISOR and Assembly101, with qualitative analysis on ARCTIC.; Reported HO-F1 gains range from 4.4 to 9.5 percentage points against the principal controlled baselines.

TL;DR: A dynamic hypergraph model represents time-varying multi-entity manipulation configurations and substantially improves fine-grained activity recognition over matched pairwise-graph and static-hypergraph baselines.

Problem: Fine-grained manipulation recognition must track evolving relations among hands, objects, tools, and surfaces, whereas conventional pairwise graphs can split coordinated multi-entity events into disconnected binary interactions.

Method: At each video timestep, the framework encodes entities using appearance, spatial, motion, and semantic-role features; constructs and ranks candidate hyperedges using proximity, contact, and motion-coupling predicates; applies node-to-hyperedge and hyperedge-to-node message passing; and uses temporal attention to reason over the evolving interaction structure.

Key contribution: The main novelty is dynamic higher-order relational modeling: coordinated configurations of multiple entities are represented as time-varying hyperedges rather than pairwise edges or a static hypergraph. It also produces class-agnostic importance scores for configurations and time intervals, explicitly presented as model emphasis rather than causal explanation.

Main result: Under an annotation-assisted entity-localization protocol, the method improves HO-F1 over a matched pairwise graph by 6.9 percentage points on EPIC-KITCHENS-100/VISOR and 9.5 points on Assembly101; gains over a static hypergraph are 4.4 and 5.8 points, respectively. Qualitative ARCTIC results associate highly ranked hyperedges with contact-rich intervals.

Limitation: The evaluation relies on annotation-assisted entity localization, so end-to-end performance with automatically detected entities is unclear. The abstract reports no statistical uncertainty, ablation study, robustness analysis, efficiency results, code release, or evidence that importance scores are causal explanations.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query "causality" is low: the paper concerns interpretable importance scoring in activity recognition and explicitly warns that its scores are not causal explanations, but it does not propose or evaluate a causal inference method.

Tags: human activity recognition, computer vision, dynamic hypergraphs, higher-order relations, video understanding, manipulation recognition, temporal attention, interpretability

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing entity feature extraction, predicate-based dynamic hyperedge construction, bidirectional hypergraph message passing, temporal attention, and the resulting importance scores would be useful.

item.description:

TL;DR: This paper introduces causal-formula verification—testing whether a specified observational formula identifies an interventional distribution—and proposes a falsifier-based verifier plus a gateway test for front-door admissibility.

Problem: Standard causal identification asks whether some identifying formula exists; this work studies the distinct problem of deciding whether a particular proposed observational formula is identifying for a target intervention.

Method: The authors formalize verification in causal graphical models, propose a falsifier, prove it yields an almost-surely correct verifier for regular exponential-family models, and derive the gateway test for front-door formulas.

Key contribution: It separates verification from identification, argues that sound and complete identification procedures do not solve verification, and supplies a practical falsifier-based route with a front-door-specific admissibility test.

Main result: The abstract claims an almost-sure correctness guarantee for the verifier under regular exponential-family models and that the gateway test finds all sets admissible for use in a front-door formula.

Limitation: The supplied abstract gives no experimental setup, quantitative results, comparison baselines, implementation details, code/data link, or evidence about behavior beyond the stated model class.

Relevance: Directly relevant to the matched query "causality": it advances causal graphical-model methodology for validating proposed interventional estimands.

TL;DR: An observational, adoption-timed study of 2,991 open-source projects finds that first-bot adoption coincides with stronger coordination, fewer conflict cascades, and more distinctive outputs, while explicitly stopping short of causal claims.

Problem: Whether automated agents joining human teams strengthen or weaken a group’s organizational capacity and outcomes.

Method: The authors analyze public GitHub interactions for two years before and after each project’s first bot adoption, measuring repeated engagement, social memory, role differentiation, conflict cascades, and output distinctiveness.

Key contribution: It frames bots as social participants rather than tools and argues that adoption-associated changes operate through organizational capabilities, separating coordination-related from differentiation-related mechanisms.

Main result: Bot adoption is followed by more repeated collaboration, greater recognition of specific bots, fewer conflict cascades, and more distinctive outputs; these shifts cluster around adoption rather than appearing gradually.

Limitation: The abstract states there is no untreated comparison group, so findings are precisely timed associations rather than causal effects; it provides no abstract-level evidence on robustness checks, ablations, code, or data release.

Relevance: Directly relevant to the “causality” query because the paper studies adoption-timed associations and is unusually explicit about the limits of causal interpretation without a control group.

item.title: Analogical Deep Research: Retrieving and Integrating Historical Analogies for Foresight Analysis
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 60, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations, h-index, and citation signals, DOI, venue, journal, or conference validation, Benchmark size, data sources, split design, and exact metrics, Named baselines, ablations, statistical significance, and robustness analysis, Code repository, implementation details, and public data availability

Score evidence: Introduces ADR and ADR-bench for historical-analogy-based foresight.; Attributes existing LLM-agent failures to surface-feature matching rather than mechanism matching.; Reports up to 10% improvement and superiority over state-of-the-art deep-research agents on ADR-bench.; The metadata identifies this as arXiv v1 and labels it an ongoing project.

TL;DR: CANA is an LLM-agent framework for foresight research that uses causal structure to retrieve and integrate historical analogies rather than relying on superficial similarity.

Problem: LLM research agents struggle to identify useful historical analogies for foresight because they tend to match surface features instead of the underlying mechanisms that produced events.

Method: The paper introduces Analogical Deep Research (ADR) and ADR-bench, then proposes Causal Analogical Researcher (CANA): an agentic framework using structural decomposition plus structural feedback for reflective analogy identification and integration. Its stated principles are mechanism alignment and cross-analogy confirmation.

TL;DR: This paper proposes a structured multi-agent pipeline for causal root-cause analysis of multimodal microservice telemetry and argues that current systems are limited primarily by model-level reasoning rather than evidence extraction.

Problem: Root-cause identification from large-scale production telemetry combining metrics, logs, and traces, evaluated on the difficult OpenRCA benchmark.

Method: A Structured Multi-Agent RCA pipeline operating with either injected domain knowledge or knowledge-free inputs; it adds reverse reasoning to classify errors as reasoning gaps versus data ambiguity, then mines discrimination rules automatically from those reports.

Key contribution: The paper diagnoses RCA failures by testing whether the necessary anomaly evidence was available but unused, and uses those diagnoses to automate rule mining instead of relying solely on manual knowledge curation.

Main result: The abstract reports that the proposed pipeline substantially outperforms existing LLM-based and classical baselines, and that evidence is present in the vast majority of failures; no numerical results are provided in the supplied metadata.

Limitation: The supplied abstract gives no accuracy values, per-configuration breakdowns, statistical tests, ablations, code link, implementation details, or external-benchmark validation. Its conclusion that model-level improvement is required is therefore not independently assessable from the provided material.

Relevance: Directly relevant to the "causal discovery" feed: it compares classical causal discovery with LLM-based reasoning for causal root-cause analysis and explicitly studies why those approaches fail on multimodal telemetry.

item.title: Live Gurbani Tracking: A Benchmark and Reference System for Captioning Sikh Kirtan
item.description:
Priority: 59/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 39, Paper 53, Empirical 63, Reproducibility 80, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: Author affiliation, citation count, and h-index are not supplied., No DOI, venue, journal reference, or external validation is supplied., No non-trivial competitive baselines are described in the abstract., No component ablations, confidence intervals, statistical tests, or robustness analyses are described., Public repository URL, data-access details, and implementation details beyond the high-level pipeline are not supplied.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 with no DOI or journal/conference reference supplied.; The abstract specifies four hand-annotated recordings, 12 evaluation cases, and about 57 minutes of scored audio.; It reports 57.9% frame accuracy and 10/12 correct shabad locks for live × blind.; The benchmark, scorer, reference system, live deployment, and on-device app are stated to be released under permissive licenses.

TL;DR: Introduces a benchmark and efficient reference system for causally tracking and displaying exact canonical Sikh scripture lines during live Kirtan performances.

Problem: Live Kirtan captioning requires selecting the exact canonical (shabad_id, line_idx) at each moment, where transcription errors are unacceptable; standard ASR transcription metrics do not directly measure this display-correctness objective.

Method: The paper formalizes live/offline and blind/oracle task variants, releases a 12-case annotated benchmark and frame-level scorer, and provides a pipeline of fine-tuned 120M IndicConformer ASR, fuzzy canonical-text matching, and a state machine, deployed with INT8 ONNX inference.

Key contribution: It reframes captioning as closed-vocabulary canonical-line tracking rather than open-vocabulary lyric transcription, with explicit causal (live) evaluation and a released benchmark, scorer, reference implementation, and live deployment.

Main result: On the hardest live × blind setting, the reference system achieves 57.9% overall 1-second frame accuracy across 12 evaluation cases and correctly locks onto the shabad in 10 of 12 cases; reported runtime is approximately RTF 0.05 on one Apple Silicon core.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to four annotated recordings (~57 minutes; 12 offset-based cases). The abstract reports only trivial baselines and does not establish robustness across performers, recording conditions, repertoire, alternative ASR systems, or ablations of pipeline components.

Relevance: Relevant to the "causality" feed because the benchmark explicitly distinguishes live causal inference from offline full-audio access and evaluates the difficult causal live-tracking setting.

Tags: causal inference, live captioning, speech recognition, closed-vocabulary retrieval, benchmark, Sikh Kirtan, on-device inference

Figure note: A task-matrix and reference-pipeline figure would be useful: live versus offline and blind versus oracle variants, followed by IndicConformer ASR, fuzzy matcher, state machine, and canonical line display.

item.description:

Score evidence: The abstract explicitly frames label-selection bias and builds on SAR-PU propensity weighting.; It proposes normalized inverse-probability-weighted PU risk and deep propensity-score estimation.; It reports experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and ADNI against several PU baselines.; The metadata identifies this as an extended arXiv version of a NeurIPS 2023 paper.

TL;DR: PUe improves positive-unlabeled classification under selection-biased labeling by using normalized propensity scores and normalized inverse-probability weighting.

Problem: Standard cost-sensitive PU methods often assume observed positives are selected at random, an assumption violated when labeling probability varies across examples.

Method: The paper builds on the SAR-PU propensity-weighted framework with a normalized inverse-probability-weighted PU risk, regularized deep propensity-score estimation, theoretical error analysis, and integration with cost-sensitive PU methods; it also allows selectively labeled negative classes.

Key contribution: It adapts causal-inference-style propensity weighting to biased PU learning through normalization, aiming to reduce instability or error from biased label-selection probabilities.

Main result: On MNIST, CIFAR-10, and ADNI under non-uniform label distributions, PUe reportedly improves over several PU baselines.

Limitation: The abstract provides no effect sizes, uncertainty estimates, ablations, code/data availability, or details on robustness to propensity-model misspecification; the causal claims appear focused on correcting selection bias rather than establishing causal effects.

Relevance: Directly relevant to causal-inference tracking because it applies propensity scores and inverse-probability weighting to correct selection bias in PU-learning labels.

Tags: causal inference, propensity scores, inverse probability weighting, selection bias, positive-unlabeled learning, PU learning, cost-sensitive learning

Figure note: No direct figure URL is supplied. A useful image would be the PUe pipeline showing biased label selection, propensity-score estimation, normalized weighting, and the downstream PU risk.

TL;DR: OrDA uses orthogonal representation disentanglement and causal inference to separate genuine content interest from position-driven access habits in homepage marketing recommendations.

Problem: Homepage marketing clicks mix content interest with habitual access/position effects, creating pseudo-positives that bias learned recommendation signals.

Method: A dual-tower model with a gated feature-allocation layer learns separate interest and habit representations; orthogonal regularization makes their latent manifolds perpendicular, and do-calculus intervention ranks items using the purified interest score at inference.

Key contribution: The paper combines adaptive feature routing, explicit geometric orthogonality, and causal intervention to target access-habit bias in marketing-slot recommendation.

Main result: The abstract reports better predictive accuracy than state-of-the-art methods and a 5.64% UCTR improvement in an online A/B test on Zhima homepage marketing-block/rent-floor recommendations.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, baselines, metrics beyond UCTR, A/B-test duration or statistical significance, ablations, robustness checks, code, or data availability.

Relevance: Directly relevant to the "causality" query: it explicitly uses causal intervention to debias recommendation rankings under behavioral and positional confounding.
